coredata.dc:title: Dual frequency cavitation event sensor with iodide dosimeter
coredata.dc:creator: Ebrahiminia, Ali
Mokhtari-Dizaji, Manijhe
Toliyat, Tayebeh
coredata.dc:description: The inertial cavitation activity depends on the sonication parameters. The purpose of this work is development of dual frequency inertial cavitation meter for therapeutic applications of ultrasound waves. In this study, the chemical effects of sonication parameters in dual frequency sonication (40kHz and 1MHz) were investigated in the progressive wave mode using iodide dosimetry. For this purpose, efficacy of different exposure parameters such as intensity, sonication duration, sonication mode, duty factor and net ultrasound energy on the inertial cavitation activity have been studied. To quantify cavitational effects, the KI dosimeter solution was sonicated and its absorbance at a wavelength of 350nm was measured. The absorbance values in continuous sonication mode was significantly higher than the absorbance corresponding to the pulsed mode having duty factors of 20–80% (p <0.05). Among different combination modes (1MHz100% +40kHz100%, 1MHz100% +40kHz80%, 1MHz80% +40kHz100%, 1MHz80% +40kHz80%), the continuous mode for dual frequency sonication is more effective than other combinations (p <0.05). The absorbance for this combined dual frequency mode was about 1.8 times higher than that obtained from the algebraic summation of single frequency sonications. It is believed that the optimization of dual frequency sonication parameters at low-level intensity (<3W/cm2) by optically assisted cavitation event sensor can be useful for ultrasonic treatments.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dual frequency
Sonication
Acoustic cavitation
Iodide dosimeter

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) KI dosimeter arrangement for dual frequency sonication, 40kHz (∗) and 1MHz (∗∗). Probes attached to the cubic cell of sonication (#), micro-thermocouple (##), and (b) schematic view of the cross section of tank with reaction cell and transducers in proportion to the scale.
Temperature profile during sonication with dual-frequency fields at 0.5–2.0W/cm2 for 1MHz and 1.34W/cm2 for 40kHz.
Mean±SD of absorbance versus PRF of 40kHz sonication (20min, 1.34W/cm2). The absorbance value was measured for five independent runs (CV=6–14%).
Absorbance values versus sonication time in continuous mode for 1MHz (2W/cm2), 40kHz (1.34W/cm2) and dual frequency sonications. Error bars are the standard deviation of mean values.

Dependence of absorbance on sonication intensity for the continuous mode of combined dual frequency sonication (1MHz, I =0.5–2.0W/cm2 and 40kHz, I =1.34W/cm2, 20min). Absorbance value curve in terms of intensity (dotted line) and the trend line on the curve using linear regression analysis (solid line) are shown. T =22–23°C and CV=7–13%.
The synergistic and the additive absorbance values for dual frequency sonication in various combinations of sonication modes. Error bars are standard deviation.
Mean±SD of the absorbance values resulted from 1MHz (I SATA =2W/cm2) and 40kHz (I SATA =1.34W/cm2) sonication at different duty cycles (sonication duration 20min).
Mean±SD of absorbance values obtained from 1MHz sonication at different duty cycles compensated for duration (I SATA =2W/cm2, 12,000J) and intensity (20min, 6000J).

head.ce:title: Dual frequency cavitation event sensor with iodide dosimeter

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Medical Physics, Faculty of Medical Sciences, Tarbiat Modares University, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutics, Faculty of Pharmacy, Tehran University of Medical Sciences, Tehran, Iran

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Medical Physics, Tarbiat Modares University, Tehran, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The inertial cavitation activity depends on the sonication parameters. The purpose of this work is development of dual frequency inertial cavitation meter for therapeutic applications of ultrasound waves. In this study, the chemical effects of sonication parameters in dual frequency sonication (40kHz and 1MHz) were investigated in the progressive wave mode using iodide dosimetry. For this purpose, efficacy of different exposure parameters such as intensity, sonication duration, sonication mode, duty factor and net ultrasound energy on the inertial cavitation activity have been studied. To quantify cavitational effects, the KI dosimeter solution was sonicated and its absorbance at a wavelength of 350nm was measured. The absorbance values in continuous sonication mode was significantly higher than the absorbance corresponding to the pulsed mode having duty factors of 20–80% (p <0.05). Among different combination modes (1MHz100% +40kHz100%, 1MHz100% +40kHz80%, 1MHz80% +40kHz100%, 1MHz80% +40kHz80%), the continuous mode for dual frequency sonication is more effective than other combinations (p <0.05). The absorbance for this combined dual frequency mode was about 1.8 times higher than that obtained from the algebraic summation of single frequency sonications. It is believed that the optimization of dual frequency sonication parameters at low-level intensity (<3W/cm2) by optically assisted cavitation event sensor can be useful for ultrasonic treatments.

ce:section.ce:para: Several researches have been carried out using multiple frequencies to enhance cavitation events [8,9]. Multiple frequency ultrasound is increasingly being employed to achieve enhancement of sonochemical reaction performance by the superimposition of ultrasound fields [6,10,11]. It has been suggested that ultrasonic fields of a few tens of kHz cause main cavitation activities in the medium while the MHz fields lead to nucleation of bubbles which in combination with kHz frequency results in cavitation activity increase [6]. Therefore, we believe that by combining 40kHz and 1MHz frequencies, it is possible to enhance cavitation and concentrate energy in a restricted area in a media such as human tissue. Numerous studies have been carried out of dual frequency enhanced cavitation activity, however, the experimental settings and conditions differ from one another and a universal setting protocol is not available. In our previous studies, it has been shown that a combination of two ultrasonic fields including a kHz plus a MHz continuous field has the highest subharmonic amplitude and the most localized effective irradiation area [6].

In this study, the effect of different sonication parameters for the mentioned combined field was optically assessed in several combinations; mode of sonication, duty cycle, intensity and duration at low level intensities (I ⩽3W/cm2) by dual frequency cavitation event sensor. To quantify cavitational effects, the KI dosimeter solution was sonicated and its absorbance at a wavelength of 350nm was measured for I3 − spectrophotometrically. The purpose of this work is development of dual frequency inertial cavitation meter for therapeutic applications of ultrasound waves. This method can be defined as optically assisted dual frequency cavitation event sensor.

Cavitational activity were estimated under various sonication conditions using a potassium iodide (KI) dosimeter as a standard method [7,12–14]. In this method, when potassium iodide (KI) solution is sonicated, oxidation occurs and I− ions are oxidized by the generated reactive radicals to give I2. The excess of I− ions present in the solution reacts with I2 to form triiodide ion (I3 −) [11]. The concentration of I3 − ions can be quantified via UV spectrophotometer at 350.00nm [13]. When a potassium iodide solution is sonicated, the following reactions occur [15] (reactions 1–5):
(Reaction 1)
OH +
I
-
→
OH
-
+ I
(Reaction 2)
H
2
O
2
+ 2
I
-
+ 2 H + →
I
2
+ 2
H
2
O
(Reaction 3)
I +
I
-
→ I
2
-
(Reaction 4)
2
I
2
-
→
I
2
+ 2
I
-
(Reaction 5)
I
2
+
I
-
→
I
3
-

Indeed, this is an indirect method for inertial cavitation activity estimation in ultrasound fields. In these experiments, a modified iodide dosimeter was prepared from a 0.1M potassium iodide solution (KI, 16.6g, Merck, Darmstadt, Germany) and then 0.2M chloral hydrate (CCl3CH (OH)2, 33g, Merck, Darmstadt, Germany) was added to the dosimeter solution. Chloral hydrate was added to enhance the oxidation of iodide [8]. Double distilled water was used as a solvent for experiments and resultant solution was made up 1 dm3. Sonication was carried out at 22°C before which, dosimeter solution was aerated for 15min. In all experiments, 50ml of KI solution were added to the reaction cell [12]. The sonicated dosimeter was analyzed for I3 − by spectrophotometer (Optizen 2120 UV Plus spectrometer, Mecasys Co Ltd., Daejeon, Korea). The absorbance was measured in the individual samples immediately after sonication at 350nm spectrophotometry.

The applied MHz ultrasound system was a 1MHz therapeutic probe (Sonopuls 492, Enrof Nonius Co., Rotterdam, Netherland) with a 30mm diameter and 5cm2 effective radiation area (ERA). By this 1MHz apparatus, it was possible to change Duty factor (20–80%), sonication mode and intensity (0–2W/cm2). For 40kHz sonication, a system was designed and constructed with a probe diameter of 30mm in our lab (Tarbiat Modares University, Ultrasound Lab, Tehran, Iran) in collaboration with Pars Nahand Engineering Company (Pardis Technology Park, Tehran, Iran). This system was capable of working either in continues or pulsating mode within a range of different duty factors (20–80%), different intensity (0–1.34W/cm2) and also, at a wide range of pulse repetition frequencies (PRF) from 0.5Hz to several kHz. All reported experimental intensity values include the spatial average temporal average intensity (I SATA). Acoustic frequency and intensity calibration of the applied systems was performed in degassed water in a cubic 25×20×20cm3 Perspex tank. The inner surfaces of the tank in front of the transducers were covered by ultrasonic absorbent material to reduce acoustical reflections. A calibrated PVDF-type hydrophone (PA124, Precision Acoustics Ltd., Dorchester, Dorset, UK, calibration range: 20kHz–1MHz, with a sensor diameter of 25mm) was positioned at a distance of 5mm from the probe surface on the transducer axis to record the ultrasonic signals at the place of chemical dosimetry chamber. The hydrophone was connected to a digital oscilloscope and spectrum analyzer (TNM 20080, TNM Electronics Ltd., Tehran, Iran) and then to the computer (Intel Pentium IV, 3.00GHz, Taiwan) for the observation, measurement and processing of the spectrum [16]. Actual frequencies of the therapeutic and our designed probes were 980.95kHz was 39.5kHz respectively. In order to compare the effect of the sonication mode at different duty factors (DF%), having equal ultrasound energy, on the inertial cavitation activity, the ultrasound energy (E) was defined as [10]:
(1)
E
( J ) = I
( W /
cm
2
) × t
( s ) × D . F
( % ) × ERA
(
cm
2
)
where I, t and ERA are spatial average temporal average intensity, exposure duration and effective radiation area respectively. The sonication field was compensated for duration and intensity in 1MHz frequency pulsed wave mode to maintain the ultrasound energy constant. The compensation processes were as following. According to the Eq. (1), the ultrasound energy transferred to the sonicated solution is proportional to the ultrasound intensity (SATA), exposure duration, ERA and duty factor. For 1MHz probe, the ultrasound intensity was set on 2W/cm2. Then the duty factor (DF) was changed from 20% to 100% (20, 50, 80 and 100) and sonication duration was changed accordingly to compensate the effect of changing DF and maintain the transferred energy constant (1200J).

For the next experiment, the sonication duration was set on 20min and again the DF values were changed from 50% to 100% (50, 80,100) and the intensity (SATA) was modified accordingly (2, 1.3 and 1W/cm2 respectively). This way, the net ultrasonic energy transferred to the sonicated solution remained constant (6000J).

For sonication, a cubic reaction cell with a volume of 50cm3 was constructed from Perspex and attached to the inner surface of the cubic 15×15×10cm3 Perspex tank. In order to maximize the acoustic intensity entering the reaction cell, the ultrasonic transducers were attached directly to the reaction cell with an orthogonal position (Fig. 1 ).

In single frequency sonication, one of the probes was used whereas both probes were used in dual-frequency sonication. The experiments were performed under progressive-wave conditions and the inner surfaces of the reaction cell in front of the probes were covered by ultrasound absorbent material to reduce acoustical reflections. The tank was filled with degassed water and the temperature of the dosimeter solution was recorded using a digital microthermocouple (Multilogger Thermometer CHY502A, Hsin-Chu, Taiwan), and no change of more than 10°C was noted in dual-frequency fields at 0.5–2.0W/cm2 for 1MHz and 1.34W/cm2 for 40kHz (Fig. 2 ).

In the present study, the cavitation activity in dual-frequency fields (40kHz and 1MHz) at low level intensity (<3W/cm2) was the subject of investigation to determine the influence of the sonication parameters in reactive radical production. Using the iodide dosimetry method which is the standard method of cavitation measurement, we evaluated the effects of various sonication parameters related to the sonication settings and their efficacies on the inertial cavitation activity in ultrasound fields by monitoring reactive radicals. For this purpose, efficacy of different exposure parameters such as intensity (0.5–2.0W/cm2 for 1MHz and 1.34W/cm2 for 40kHz), sonication duration (10–60min), sonication mode (continuous and pulsating), duty factor (20–80%) and net ultrasound energy on the inertial cavitation activity has been studied.

According to the guidelines established by the Journal of Chromatography B, at least five replicates at each measurement are required for the precision to be within 15% relative standard deviation (RSD) [17]. Therefore, in the present study, measurement of the absorbance was performed at least five runs and summary statistics for all normal distributed variables are presented as mean±standard deviation (SD). After normal distribution and homogeny variance verification, ANOVA statistical analysis with a significant level of 0.05 (p <0.05) was carried out. Linear regression analysis between absorbance and ultrasonic parameters was carried out and the correlation coefficients were estimated. Reproducibility of experiments was presented as the percentage of coefficient of the variance (CV). The statistical analysis was performed using the SPSS 16 software package (SPSS V.16, Inc., Chicago, IL, USA).

The results of sonochemical absorbance values attributed to 40kHz and 1MHz ultrasound fields having duty factors ranging from 20% to 100% (20%, 50%, 80% and 100%) are given in Table 1 . As it can be seen from Table 1, for different duty cycles, the absorbance values of the continuous sonication mode is significantly higher than that of the pulse mode (p <0.05).

In Table 2 , the absorbance values resulted from sonication having different duty cycles compensated for sonication duration (I SATA =2W/cm2) and intensity (20min) are presented. If the net ultrasound energy remains constant for compensated duration (Eq. (1)), the absorbance values attributed to duty cycles of 80%, 50% and 20% is are not significantly different with each other (p >0.05) while they are significantly different compared to that of continuous mode (p <0.05). It can be considered from Table 2 that in sonications compensated for intensity, the absorbance values in all duty factors, are approximately equal and differences between are not significant (p >0.05).

Temporal changes of the reactive radical production in dosimeter solution for single and dual frequency sonication were examined over 60min with 10min steps. The absorption spectra of dosimeter solution at single and dual frequency sonication in various durations were recorded. Results show that the dual frequency duration is effective in sonochemical reaction. The absorbance values versus sonication duration in 1MHz, 40kHz and dual frequency sonications are shown in Fig. 4 . The absorbance increased by increasing the sonication duration at single or dual frequency sonication. The results show that the dual frequency duration is effective in sonochemical reaction.

It is well known that KI dosimeter absorbance increases by increasing the sonication intensity [16,28]. That is, ultrasound intensity is an important parameter in sonochemical experiments. In the above experiments, the intensity of 40kHz frequency was constant (I SATA =1.34W/cm2) but the intensity of 1MHz frequency was gradually increased (0.5, 1.0, 1.5 and 2.0W/cm2). A series (including five independent runs) of 20min sonications were carried out and the average of resulted absorbance values were plotted versus the ultrasound intensity (Fig. 5 ). As expected, the cavitation activity increased at higher intensities which lead to an increase in absorbance values. The results show that there is a significant correlation between absorbance and sonication intensity (R =0.99, p =0.009).

In Fig. 6 , the absorbance values are shown for combined 40kHz (I SATA =1.34W/cm2) and 1MHz (I SATA =2W/cm2) ultrasound sonication at different combination of continuous mode (100%) and pulsed mode (80%). This experiment shows that combined dual frequency ultrasound can significantly increase the inertial activity as compared to the algebraic summation of two individual sonications. Among different mode combinations, the continuous mode for dual frequency sonication is more effective than other combinations (p <0.05). The absorbance for this combined dual frequency mode was about 1.8 times higher than that obtained from the algebraic summation of single frequency sonications.

To quantify the cavitation area, a thin aluminum foil (0.02mm) was placed at a distance of 20mm from the surfaces of 40kHz and 1MHz frequency ultrasonic probes with an angle of 45°. No foil damage was seen at both sonications.

Ultrasound waves penetrate the tissue and focus energy on deep tumors. In addition, it has been suggested that the non-thermal effects of ultrasound waves such as acoustic cavitation can play a key role in cancer treatment [18,19]. Chemical dosimeters such as iodide dosimeter give the direct sonochemical efficiency, produce reproducible results and are widely acceptable [3,7]. Therefore, in this research the amount of reactive radicals was detected using an iodide dosimeter as an indicator of the degree of inertial cavitation.

The experimental results of the literature revealed that dual frequency sonication is capable of significantly enhancing the acoustic cavitation effects compared to the single frequency sonication [20–22]. Potential application of dual frequency enhanced acoustic cavitation in sonodynamic therapy, [18,23,24] sonochemistry enhancement [11,25] and drug delivery has been widely proposed [26].

It is essential to calibrate and maintain the experimental acoustic field and conditions. In the present study, different setting parameters for single and multi frequency sonication are investigated that how they affect the inertial cavitation efficiency.

Therefore, the purpose of this study was to investigate the effects of sonication parameters of dual frequency ultrasound waves on induced cavitation. In the present study, the absorbance change of KI dosimeter caused by reactive radicals in different setting parameters of 40kHz and 1MHz single and dual frequency sonications was evaluated and compared. The dual frequency sonication enhanced cavitation activity significantly compared to the single frequency mode. The results confirmed that, under the conditions of the present study, the combination mode of sonication caused an absorbance enhancement of about 1.8 times higher than that obtained with the algebraic sum of the 40kHz and 1MHz sources and also 3 times the single 1MHz sonication alone. Also, as shown in Fig. 4, the temporal evolution of iodine release under combined sonication indicates a synergistic effect with sonication duration increase.

The absorbance values increased in higher duty factor (80%) and for continuous mode (100%) is higher than the pulsed mode (0.39±0.03 and 0.32±0.03, respectively). Moreover, our experimental data show that by increasing the PRF from 0.5 to 5Hz at 40kHz sonication, the absorbance decreases (0.23±0.03 and 0.10±0.01, respectively). As a result, the sonochemical effects depend to net ultrasound energy.

Liu et al. [2] found that combined mode of sonication (83+241kHz) can enhance the generation of bubbles and hydrogen peroxide compared to the single-frequency ultrasound stimulation. Feng et al. [27] studied sonochemical and electrical conductivity behavior of cavitation by using combined dual frequency sonication (28kHz and 870kHz) relative to single frequency sonication. They revealed that the application of dual frequency conditions could produce a significant increase in cavitation efficiency compared to the single frequency irradiation. Barati et al. [10], using terephthalic acid dosimetry, revealed that, the dual frequency sonication resulted in generating a fluorescence intensity approximately 1.5 times higher than that of the algebraic summation of the 150kHz and 1MHz sonications and also 3.5 times higher than the intensity due to single sonication. Hasanzadeh et al. [6] used different sonication frequencies (28kHz, 130kHz, 1MHz, 3MHz) and dual combinations to investigate acoustic cavitation. Measurements of subharmonic amplitude showed that combination of kHz and MHz frequencies could significantly increase the inertial cavitation activity.

Although the combined frequencies used in the present study were not the same as those of the mentioned studies, but an enhancement in cavitation yield was observed in this study too. Buldakov et al. [28], using a 1MHz frequency probe at low intensities (<3W/cm2), examined the impact of changing PRF (from 0.5 to 100Hz) on chemical effects via electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) method. They reported that the lowest free radical formation was found at 5Hz. In comparison with this work, an agreement exists with the absorbance value at 40kHz frequency for KI solution depicted in Fig. 3. In the case of cavitation area, no destructed foil was seen in our study, although Hasanzadeh et al. [6] reported an erosion in aluminum foil at 28kHz (0.04W/cm2) sonication. Therefore, it can be concluded that erosion effects of ultrasound are frequency dependent. The most acceptable mechanism of enhanced cavitational effect by dual frequency sonication was that the resultant field in dual frequency sonication covers a considerably wider range of frequencies than that of the sum of the individual fields [2]. The studies carried out in sonochemistry verified that the bubbles generated at higher frequency act as nuclei for lower frequency cavitation. Experimental reports support that simultaneous dual frequency sonication could disturb the sample solution to greater extent than single frequency ultrasound and may contribute to more cavitation nuclei generation. Furthermore, application of dual frequency sonication would give much more efficient mass transfer than any individual sonication, thus enhance the acoustic cavitation activity.

Kinoshita et al. [29] investigated the effect of experimental settings on sonoporation in in vitro experiments on C166 cultured cells. They used a 1.706MHz continuous wave mode planer ultrasound transducer having surface area of 7cm2 and duty factor of 100%. Results of the mentioned study showed that sonoporation efficiency increased by increasing either acoustic power from 0 to 4W/cm2 or exposure time from 15 to 120s. Despite the differences of sonication fields, our results are in agreement with that of Kinoshita et al.

In the present study, the effect of bubble clustering was not considered. Segebarth et al. [30] doped the sonicated aqueous solution with a small amount of sodium dodecyl sulfate (DSD) which acts as a surfactant and covers the bubble surface and hinders bubble clustering by inducing an electrostatic repulsion between bubbles. Their study showed that the cavitation yield is affected and increased by the surfactant. They found high correlation between the results obtained by sonoluminescence, sonochemistry and noise spectrum of the doped sonicated solution.

They also examined the cavitation temperature variations by doping the sonicated solution with SDS via the Fricke dosimeter and cavitation noise spectrum [31]. Sonication was performed using a single ultrasound field of 518MHz with a continuous mode and changeable intensity between 0 and 25W. Their results showed that SDS did not affect the pressure/temperature conditions of the cavitation but the number of active bubbles was modified by doping with SDS. Although it has been reported that solution temperature has a limited influence on the KI dosimeter results, in our study, sonicated did not changed the temperature of the solution markedly.

Chen et al. [32] examined the amount of hemolysis production enhancement by exposure to 1.5 HIFU with various pulse length and constant acoustic energy. Results of their study showed that sonication by long pulses produces much more hemolysis than short pulses (60% and 10% respectively) which is in agreement with our result showing an increase in cavitation yield by increasing quality factor.

This study suffers from some limitation, which should be addressed. In the present study, the combined fields were generated using two separated probes. This may lead to phase difference in the interfered field, which has not been considered yet. Liu et al. [2] proposed a single transducer to generate dual frequency sonication. It is proposed to evaluate the effect of different sonication conditions using single probe capable of generating dual frequency fields. Moreover, it would be of great interest to investigate the effect of phase difference of the ultrasound fields superimposed as a sonication parameter in the future studies.

In the present study, because of equipment limitations, it was not possible to change the sonication intensities in a wide range. Therefore, the lowest intensity under which the cavitation was detectable, could not be extracted. This limitation should be considered in future studies using a wide range of intensities.

This study demonstrated that dual frequency sonication (40kHz+1MHz) could give sonochemical yield 1.8 times larger than that of the individual sonication summation. It is believed that the optimization of dual frequency sonication parameters at low-level intensity (<3W/cm2) can be useful for sonodynamic therapy.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was approved by Tarbiat Modares University. This work was supported in part by the Iran National Science Foundation (INSF).
